aesthetic: a grand old Edwardian theatre, long shut up and faded, and the occult warren of tunnels beneath it — faded gilt, dusty velvet, cobwebs and old playbills; atmospheric, theatrical and a touch spooky — sensational and melodramatic rather than grim, oppressive or genuinely menacing. The upper theatre is dry and dusty, NOT wet; dampness, weeping brick and standing water belong only to the cellars, tunnels and sewers far below, where the prose calls for them. The place is abandoned and unpowered: the lights are DEAD. The chandelier, wall sconces, lamps and any candles are UNLIT, cold and dark — no flames, no glowing wicks, no electric glow, no warm pools of light; illumination is only dim, cold, grey ambient daylight leaking through grimy windows and gaps, leaving deep shadow. (Living candlelight or firelight appears ONLY where the prose explicitly places an active flame — e.g. a lit ritual deep in the cellars — never as ambient set-dressing.)

scenes.theatre-lobby: Interior of a vast, opulent Edwardian theatre lobby — a cavernous double-height entrance hall, far wider and taller than it is deep, the grand foyer of a once-prestigious theatre. An open central void rises the full two storeys to an ornate plastered ceiling. Viewed from the main entrance (behind the viewpoint and out of frame) looking across the floor toward the far wall. The floor is a plain, worn marble-flagged foyer floor in muted neutral stone tones — a finished hard floor, NOT bare ground or dirt; understated, not brightly patterned. The focal point, centred on the far wall, is a monumental imperial (double-return) staircase whose steps run beneath a worn carpet runner: one wide central flight sweeps up to a deep mid-level landing, where it divides into two symmetric flights that curve left and right and rise to land directly on a continuous balustraded gallery. That gallery is one unbroken balcony ringing all four walls of the upper storey — running back along both side walls and across the entrance wall above the main doors — open over the floor below, so the whole upper level reads as a wraparound balcony around the central void. One upper storey only, no higher tiers. On the mid-landing wall beneath the split hangs a large faded full-length portrait of a distinguished gentleman in an old-fashioned suit. On the far side of the upper gallery, a single set of tall closed double doors leads through toward the auditorium; the rest of the gallery is plain balustrade with no other doors. At ground level the lobby has exactly two doorways, both small, private, modest panelled doors flanking the staircase: one closed door in the wall to the left, one closed door in the wall to the right — discreet and plain against the opulence of the hall, with no other ground-floor doors. A few tall theatrical playbill posters are pasted flat to the plaster, bold pictorial imagery faded but striking. Opulent gilt and grand proportions, long shut up — faded grandeur on a vast scale.
scenes.managers-office: A cluttered former manager's office, viewed from the doorway looking across the room. A heavy wooden desk strewn with loose papers stands against the far wall, with more papers scattered across the floor. A closed metal wall-safe is set in one wall. Set into the floorboards is an open trapdoor, with the top of a wooden ladder protruding from it, descending into darkness below. A second doorway is set in the wall ahead.
scenes.basement: A cellar lit only by grey daylight seeping in from small street-level windows set high on one wall, viewed from across the floor. Stacked wooden boxes and a couple of large barrels are the only contents. Set in the ceiling is an open trapdoor, with a wooden ladder descending from it down to the floor. An old electrical panel with a switch is mounted on one wall.
scenes.outside-the-theatre: A run-down city street in a decayed slum neighbourhood at twilight, viewed from the kerb looking toward the theatre's shabby frontage. Litter is strewn across the cracked pavement and a slight mist hangs low. Boarded-up, deteriorating buildings line the street, which runs away to either side into the distance. An empty parking space gapes at the kerb.
scenes.cramped-hallway: A narrow, cramped service hallway, viewed looking ahead along its length, its walls lined with stacked boxes of junk that pinch the passage tight. A doorway is set in the wall behind and another in the wall to the left; the hallway continues ahead.
scenes.tight-stairway: A narrow, winding little service stairway, viewed looking up its turn. It climbs upward ahead and descends behind. Cramped.
scenes.centre-of-long-corridor: A long corridor panelled in dark, aged wood, viewed looking along its length. A narrow little staircase descends to the right; a closed door is set in the wall to the left. Worn floorboards.
scenes.south-end-of-long-corridor: The end of a long, dark wood-panelled corridor, viewed looking along its length. A doorway in the wall behind and another in the wall to the left. Worn boards.
scenes.music-room: A cluttered former music room, viewed from the doorway looking in. Battered violin cases and tangled coils of instrument strings litter the floor. An upright piano has been pushed up against one wall. Disused.
scenes.prop-room: A storage room for stage props, viewed from the doorway looking in: shelves crammed with assorted junk line the walls. A closed trapdoor is set in the ceiling overhead. Cluttered.
scenes.southern-end-of-the-attic: The cramped far end of a dusty attic. Rough wooden studs and crossbeams obstruct the low space; cobwebs and thick dust coat everything. The attic continues ahead deeper in. An open trapdoor in the floor leads down.
scenes.attic-above-the-theatre: A dusty attic space over the theatre auditorium: bare crossbeams and a thinly plastered floor, cracks in the plaster glowing faintly with light rising from the auditorium far below. The attic continues behind. Ahead, mounted on a pair of sturdy crossbeams, stands a large winch with a hand crank; a thick cable runs from it down through a hole in the floor. Drifting dust.
scenes.attic-observatory: A bizarre attic observatory. Instead of a dome, the roof is a single, strangely patterned window. The exposed wooden wall-beams are set at unnatural, impossible angles, giving the unsettling impression of standing inside a large many-sided geometric solid. A large, oddly-shaped brass telescope stands in the centre of the room. An opening leads out.
scenes.narrow-hallway: A narrow hallway where stacked boxes have collapsed into a large heap of junk, viewed looking ahead down its length. A pile of fallen carpet rolls lies tumbled across the passage, blocking the way forward. The way back lies behind the viewpoint. Choked with debris.
scenes.end-of-hallway: The end of a narrow, box-lined hallway, viewed looking ahead. The theatre's back exit is directly ahead; the hallway continues behind the viewpoint, and an office door is set in the wall to the left. Stacked boxes line the walls.
scenes.alley-courtyard: A small enclosed courtyard at the centre of a city block, hemmed in on every side by tall, blank buildings that wall out any escape. Large old stage doors stand in the far wall, long disused. A small back entrance is set in a corner to one side. A closed metal rubbish bin stands in one corner. Grimy, open to the sky overhead.
scenes.staircase-landing: The camera stands ON the mid-landing — a flat platform — looking ahead at the far wall, which is dominated by a huge framed full-length portrait of a distinguished gentleman in an old-fashioned suit; within the painting he stands in a wood-panelled room with an oaken door and a large fireplace, its roaring fire casting flickering light, the panelled background drawn in a subtly wrong, unsettling perspective. From this landing the grand staircase SPLITS and climbs upward to either side — a balustraded flight ascends to the left and a matching flight ascends to the right, each rising straight up and out of frame toward the upper level, not curving or wrapping back on itself. These two ascending flights are the main subject. The staircase back down to the lobby is behind and below the viewpoint: show at most the topmost couple of steps at the very bottom edge of the frame, or none at all — do NOT frame the whole descending flight and do NOT place the camera at the foot of the stairs. The only ways out are the stairs themselves: no doorways, arches, or dark openings cut into the walls. Faded gilt.
scenes.secret-gallery: A cramped hidden gallery, empty but for rows of old framed pictures crowding every wall. A small door leads out.
scenes.eastern-landing: An upper landing that circles the balustraded rail above the theatre lobby, open over the lobby floor below. The landing continues in both directions along the gallery ring toward the stairs. A set of double doors in the wall ahead lead toward the auditorium; a single, finely carved wooden door is set in the wall to one side. Faded gilt.
scenes.southern-landing: The far end of the upper landing sweeping around the theatre's entrance hall, above the main doors and open over the lobby below. The landing continues in both directions along the gallery ring. Heavy closed curtains hang against the wall behind the viewpoint.
scenes.eastern-theatre-aisle: A long aisle running through a vast, wide theatre auditorium, flanked on both sides by long rows of many seats stretching far across the hall — a broad sweep of seating, not a narrow few-seats-wide strip. Viewed looking ahead up the aisle toward the far end, where a wide wooden stage stands raised above the auditorium floor, a sunken orchestra pit in front of it — a recessed trench between the stage lip and the front rows, visible from down here on the auditorium floor. A second, parallel aisle runs through the seats away to the left. Balcony boxes line the raised side walls to either side, overlooking the seating. A great crystal chandelier hangs high over the centre of the auditorium (its normal raised position, not lowered). Behind the viewpoint, at the far end of the aisle, a set of double doors leads out. Carpet runs the length of the aisle.
scenes.eastern-stairway: A finely carpeted stairway, its once-rich runner now worn, viewed looking up its rise. It descends to a wooden door below and climbs upward. Enclosed.
scenes.eastern-balcony: You are in a private balcony box, looking out over its balustrade across the cavernous auditorium. Faded plush seats, set for the former elite, fill the box. Beyond the rail the great void opens out: rows of derelict seats far below, and a great crystal chandelier hanging out over the open centre. Across the void, the matching balcony boxes are just visible. A finely-sculptured plaster ceiling spans overhead. The only way out is a low gap at the edge of the box floor — no doorway.
scenes.western-balcony: You are in a long-abandoned private balcony box, every surface under a thick layer of dust. The wall fills the frame ahead: a doorway has been bricked solid, and beside it a small ragged hole has been knocked through the wooden wall, just large enough to crawl through, opening onto darkness beyond. The box floor is a faded, dust-greyed carpet — a finished carpeted floor, not bare ground, dirt or rubble. A couple of old plush balcony chairs, set for the former elite, still stand here, shrouded in dust and cobwebs. A finely-sculptured plaster ceiling spans overhead. Behind the viewpoint, the open balcony rail overlooks the auditorium void.
scenes.back-wall: The cramped, filthy interior of a hollow space within the wall dividing the theatre from the building beside it. Bare, dirt-caked timber and brick press close on all sides; almost no light reaches in. The hollow continues in one direction, and a ragged hole opens off to one side.
scenes.wall-with-large-hole: A narrow hollow within the dividing wall, continuing ahead. A large, ragged hole has broken through to the left, opening into the neighbouring building. Dirty, confined.
scenes.secret-library: A hidden library: rows upon rows of tall bookcases packed with hundreds of old books. Aisles lead away ahead; a small recess opens behind the viewpoint. To the left a bookcase has collapsed, leaving a ragged hole broken through the wall. Enclosed.
scenes.reading-recess: A small alcove that was once a reading nook: a single small reading table and a wooden chair are all that remain. The library continues back out behind the viewpoint.
scenes.southern-end-of-the-library: The far end of the hidden library, its bookcases draped and webbed with strange, glistening pale strands. A cold, damp breeze enters through a broken French window in the wall on the left; a library aisle leads ahead. The glistening strands all converge on a large nest of rotting, heaped-up books in the centre of the floor. Damp, decaying.
scenes.street-balcony: An old stone balcony high above a side street in the abandoned quarter of the city. The view below is of boarded-up shop fronts and ruined, derelict buildings. Tall windows lead back inside behind the viewpoint; the rough outer wall rises overhead to be climbed. Weathered stone, twilight.
scenes.theatre-roof: High on the steeply sloping rooftop of the theatre, beside a large brick chimney. All around, the rooftops of the abandoned city stretch away under a deepening dusk sky. The roof falls away dangerously on every side; a safe route leads off one side toward the balcony, and the chimney mouth opens downward. Windswept.
scenes.guest-star-room: A small but opulent guest suite, its furnishings of markedly finer quality than the rest of the ruined theatre: a fine carved four-poster bed and rich, faded fittings. A fireplace is set in the wall ahead. A door leads out behind the viewpoint.
scenes.sealed-off-office: A former office, all of its furniture removed but for a small fireplace set in the wall ahead, its sooty flue an open shaft that continues both up and down. The door behind the viewpoint has been completely boarded over. In the middle of the room stands an old-fashioned wooden coffin with brass fittings, resting on a mahogany stand.
scenes.boiler-room: A cramped boiler room dominated by a huge old iron boiler, its pipes snaking out through and gripping the walls. A shaft rises from the boiler as the only way up; there is no other exit. A large, dirty coal bin stands against one wall, and a closed, grime-covered wooden chest sits nearby. Sooty.
scenes.north-end-of-long-corridor: The far end of a long, dark wood-panelled corridor, which runs away ahead into the distance, viewed looking along its length. A doorway is set in the wall behind the viewpoint and another in the wall to the right. Worn boards.
scenes.costume-room: A crowded theatrical wardrobe room packed with racks upon racks of old costumes spanning every era, from Imperial Roman dress to World War I uniforms. Three costumed display mannequins stand among them — one dressed as a pirate, one as a cowboy, one as a gladiator. A doorway leads out. Crowded.
scenes.sick-bay: A small former sick bay. The walls are covered with old, faded medical instructional posters. A single iron infirmary bed stands against one wall, and a closed medical cabinet is mounted nearby. A doorway leads out. Clinical.
scenes.sealed-up-ticket-booth: A small room that has been sealed shut, its windows boarded over so that only thin blades of faint light leak through the cracks. In one corner lies a human skeleton in a ragged, collapsed heap. Cramped.
scenes.stage: A wide wooden stage, raised well above the auditorium floor, in the derelict theatre — draped in cobwebs, viewed from the back of the stage looking out over the auditorium. The backstage area is behind the viewpoint, out of frame. The elevated stage drops away at its front lip; the sunken orchestra pit lies directly below and just in front of that lip and is HIDDEN from this raised vantage behind the stage edge — do NOT show the pit. Below and ahead spreads a vast auditorium, a sea of decaying seats, with two parallel carpeted aisles running away through it — one receding to the left, a matching one to the right. Faded balcony boxes line the raised side walls on either side. A great crystal chandelier hangs high over the centre of the auditorium. At the far end, where the aisles reach the rear wall, double doors stand as the way out — the rear exits are at the aisle ends, NOT a central door behind the seats. Cavernous.
scenes.back-stage: A chaotic back-stage area, a maze of compartments divided by sagging ropes, wooden supports and hanging curtains, cluttered with rolls of old painted canvas scenery backdrops. The stage doors ahead have been boarded over and the stairs that once climbed up have been torn down; the only way out is back through to the stage. Cluttered.
scenes.up-the-ropes: High above the stage, amid a tangle of dangling rigging ropes hanging down into the fly space. Across a narrow gap, a row of doors is set into the wall on the far side — they once opened onto a landing long since gone. One door hangs close at hand, slightly ajar.
scenes.dressing-room: A former dressing room; scuff marks on the floor are all that remain of its vanished furniture. A door leads back out. Empty.
scenes.orchestra-pit: A view from down inside a small, sunken theatre orchestra pit, in front of the stage. We stand at the bottom of the pit looking up and out toward the stage, which rises directly ahead — a broad stage front looming up above eye-level, a tattered curtain hanging above it. Close around the viewer, the low panelled pit walls rise to about chest-to-shoulder height on the left, the right, and behind; above those low walls the wide, open theatre rises and recedes. Over the rear rim, the dim auditorium opens out behind and above. Set into the right-hand pit wall is a single plain closed wooden door, at pit-floor level — a shut side door at the bottom, opening to storage cupboards. The narrow bare wooden pit floor is sunk below house level and empty. Far above, glimpsed past and above the stage lip, the tall dark fly space recedes upward into distant rows of lighting and girders. The pit is small and shallow; the theatre around and above it is wide and tall.
scenes.pit-cupboards: Dark, empty walk-in storage cupboards. The orchestra pit lies back through the door behind; a gap broken in the far wall opens into deeper darkness. Cramped.
scenes.dark-place: A pitch-dark underground space; only the faint suggestion of rough passages leading away ahead and off to one side can be made out. Near-total darkness.
scenes.under-the-stage: A cramped, low crawlspace beneath the stage; thin blades of light filter down through the cracks between the boards overhead. The ceiling is so low one must stoop. Small bits of debris that have fallen through the cracks litter the ground. A passage leads off into darkness ahead. Confined.
scenes.cloakroom: A disused cloakroom; rows of empty coat hooks line the walls, now draped only with cobwebs. The opening back out toward the lobby is visible ahead.
scenes.secret-stairway: A narrow secret stairway climbing up and descending down, feebly lit by a few bare electric bulbs set into the ceiling. Confined.
scenes.underground-passage: A dank underground passage lined with old, weeping brick walls. The passage climbs away in one direction; in the other direction it runs to a heavy, locked iron door. Damp, cold.
scenes.above-the-pit: A huge, cavern-like chamber with a large rectangular pit sunk into its floor, like a drained indoor swimming pool. A passage leads to one side through an open iron door; down inside the pit, a passage leads off in the other direction. Vast, rough stone.
scenes.inside-pit: The bottom of the rough-walled pit; its uneven sides make an easy climb back up and out. A gloomy tunnel leads away ahead into the dark. Rough stone.
scenes.tunnel-junction: A junction in a little network of rough-hewn underground tunnels. One tunnel runs back behind the viewpoint toward the pit; others branch away in three directions — ahead and to either side. Cramped, rough rock.
scenes.mine-shaft: A rough chamber resembling an old mine shaft, lit feebly by a single bare electric light. Crude rock walls; tunnels lead off in all directions. Raw stone.
scenes.deep-in-the-mines: A cavern deep in the mine workings, widened out, with signs of major past excavation. A large iron cage dominates the space. Rough rock walls.
scenes.western-end-of-sewer: The far end of a long brick sewage trench, which runs away ahead into the dark. This end terminates at a large iron sluice gate filling the wall ahead, below which a drain descends further into the muck. A passage opens in the wall to one side, leading back beneath the theatre. Wet brick.
scenes.centre-of-sewer: The middle of a long brick drainage trench, which runs off in both directions. Above, fixed high on the wall overhead, a strange metal platform juts out over the trench. Wet brick.
scenes.eastern-end-of-sewer: The far end of the drainage trench, where it dead-ends at a cluster of small, impassable pipe mouths set into the end wall. The trench runs back behind the viewpoint; a tunnel branches off to one side. Wet, grimy brick.
scenes.rat-nest: A small tunnel, its floor thick with rotting sewage and animal droppings. The tunnel ends at an unusual stone archway at the far end, beyond which lies only darkness.
scenes.metal-platform: A raised metal platform fixed high on the wall, giving a clear view down the full length of the brick sewer trench below. The trench stretches away into the distance in both directions — wet brick walls, a grimy floor. A long, rusty lever extends down from the ceiling within reach. Grimy ironwork.
scenes.witchs-lair: A small, circular chamber of red brick, domed like an igloo. In the centre stands an old iron cauldron filled with a dark, bubbling liquid. Shelves line the curved walls, crowded with little vials and strange knick-knacks. The only way out is back through a stone archway behind the viewpoint. Against the curved wall stands a large bronze statue of a four-armed woman draped in flowing bronze robes; her elongated forehead bears four eye-sockets set in a diamond pattern, and each of her four hands grips a jewelled dagger.
scenes.western-landing: An upper landing that circles the balustraded rail above the theatre lobby, open over the lobby floor below. The landing continues in both directions along the gallery. A set of double doors in the wall ahead leads toward the auditorium. Faded gilt.
scenes.western-theatre-aisle: A long aisle running the length of a vast, wide theatre auditorium, flanked on both sides by long rows of many seats stretching far across the hall — a broad sweep of seating, not a narrow few-seats-wide strip. Viewed looking up the aisle toward the far end, where a wide wooden stage stands raised above the auditorium floor, a sunken orchestra pit in front of it — a recessed trench between the stage lip and the front rows, visible from down here on the auditorium floor. A second, parallel aisle runs the same length through the seats away to the right. Balcony boxes line the raised side walls overhead, overlooking the seating. A great crystal chandelier hangs high over the centre of the auditorium in its normal raised position. Behind the viewpoint, at the far end of the aisle, a set of double doors leads out. Carpet runs the length of the aisle.
scenes.old-hallway: A sinister, murky, dim hallway running diagonally, its bare walls pressing close and the air heavy and oppressive. Viewed looking ahead into deeper gloom.
